agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier relationship with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments (>500kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of cargo to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment. Locked to a Shipment once assigned."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"The only role authorized to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment. Represents a human oversight gate."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"(Proposed) A child Shipment created by splitting an overweight parent Shipment. Does not exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"(Proposed) An automated process that decomposes a Shipment exceeding 500kg into multiple sub-shipments with independent Carrier assignments, bypassing supervisor approval."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The system guarantees every Shipment has exactly one Carrier at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. A Shipment with zero or multiple Carriers creates ambiguous accountability for cargo — who is liable, who is tracking, who delivers? The system is in a corrupt state if this is violated.","testable_assertion":"For any Shipment in any state from assignment through delivery, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting the integrity of carrier assignment. Without it, automated or unauthorized processes could silently redirect cargo, creating liability and chain-of-custody violations.","testable_assertion":"Any CarrierReassigned event must have an associated Supervisor identity in its authorization context."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment dispatched without a single accountable Carrier has no chain of custody. This is a physical constraint — cargo moves on exactly one truck at a time.","testable_assertion":"The Dispatch command must reject any Shipment where assigned_carrier is null or where multiple carriers are assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment is created at exactly 500kg","expected_behavior":"Must NOT trigger auto-split — the threshold is 'exceeds 500kg', not 'meets 500kg'. But this boundary needs explicit clarification."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is modified after creation to exceed 500kg (e.g., items added)","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Does auto-split apply retroactively to an already-assigned Shipment? If so, it forces carrier reassignment without supervisor approval — violating BR-2."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500kg after splitting","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting? The spec does not address this."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment fails delivery but others succeed","expected_behavior":"The parent Shipment is in a partial-delivery state, which does not exist in the current model. What is the Shipment status? Who is accountable?"},{"scenario":"A carrier is assigned to the original Shipment before the split occurs","expected_behavior":"The split must remove or reassign that carrier without supervisor involvement — a direct violation of BR-2."},{"scenario":"Customer disputes or insurance claims on a split shipment","expected_behavior":"Liability is ambiguous. The original Shipment contract was with one Carrier. Splitting across multiple Carriers fragments the chain of custody."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Splitting shipments across multiple carriers may increase transportation costs (multiple pickups, multiple deliveries, loss of volume discounts). Liability fragmentation increases insurance and dispute resolution costs.","operational":"Introduces a parent-child Shipment hierarchy that does not exist in the current model. Every downstream system — tracking, billing, proof of delivery, customer notifications, reporting — must be updated to handle sub-shipments. This is not a feature; it is a domain model migration.","user":"Customers who booked a single shipment will now receive multiple deliveries from multiple carriers, potentially at different times. This changes the customer experience fundamentally and may violate SLAs.","risk":"HIGH. This feature as specified directly contradicts two production invariants. Shipping it as described would corrupt the domain model. Additionally, bypassing supervisor oversight for carrier assignment removes a human safety gate that likely exists for regulatory or liability reasons.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Supervisors","impact":"Their authorization gate for carrier reassignment is bypassed. They lose visibility and control over which carriers handle which cargo."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive partial shipments they did not negotiate for. Volume, weight, and route assumptions change."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive fragmented deliveries. Tracking experience degrades. SLA accountability becomes unclear."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Must reconcile multiple carrier invoices against a single customer shipment. Revenue recognition and cost allocation become complex."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain of custody is broken across multiple carriers. Liability for damage or loss becomes ambiguous. May violate contractual obligations."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Requires introducing a Shipment hierarchy (parent/child), new lifecycle states (partial delivery), and updates to every system that assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The feature request as written directly contradicts two established production invariants (BR-1: one Carrier per Shipment, BR-2: supervisor-only reassignment). These are not ambiguities — they are contradictions. The spec also lacks critical details: What happens to the parent Shipment? How is tracking unified? What about billing? The confidence is very low because shipping this feature as described would break the domain model.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier invariant is clear and unambiguous","The bypass of supervisor authorization directly violates BR-2"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments or a new entity type","Whether the 500kg threshold is a hard physical limit or a business policy","Whether supervisor bypass is intentional or an oversight in the spec","How billing, tracking, and SLAs should work for split shipments"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are correctly stated and intentionally designed","The product team has not yet considered the domain model implications","The 500kg threshold is a business policy, not a regulatory requirement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple Carriers to what is logically a single Shipment, directly violating the production invariant that a Shipment must have exactly one Carrier (BR-1). The feature cannot coexist with the current domain model without one of them changing.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Customers","Legal"],"options":["A) Reject the feature — the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier invariant is sacrosanct","B) Evolve the domain model — introduce a ShipmentGroup or ParentShipment aggregate that contains multiple child Shipments, each preserving the 1:1 Carrier invariant. The parent is a coordination entity, not a transportable unit.","C) Relax the invariant — allow a Shipment to have multiple Carriers. This is the most dangerous option as it ripples through every downstream system."],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup) that preserves existing invariants. Each sub-shipment is a full Shipment with exactly one Carrier. The group coordinates tracking and billing. This requires significant design work but does not break the existing model."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature explicitly bypasses supervisor approval for carrier assignment, directly violating BR-2 (only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier). This removes a human oversight gate that likely exists for liability and compliance reasons.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Legal / Compliance","Operations"],"options":["A) Require supervisor approval for auto-split — defeats the 'automatic' purpose but preserves the safety gate","B) Introduce a new authorization model — auto-split is a system-level action with its own audit trail, distinct from manual reassignment. Supervisors can configure split rules but don't approve each one.","C) Remove the supervisor gate entirely — extremely risky, not recommended"],"recommendation":"Option B. Create an explicit AutoSplitPolicy that supervisors configure and approve in advance. The system executes splits within those pre-approved parameters. This preserves supervisory oversight while enabling automation."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment's cargo","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split reassigns carriers without supervisor involvement","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"The feature introduces a parent-child shipment concept that does not exist in the domain model, with undefined lifecycle semantics","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier — undefined for a 'parent' shipment that has been split"}]}
